title: Strawberry jam, open-pan method, no commercial pectin
subtitle: A pan of strawberries, sugar, lemon juice, and time.
excerpt: A pan of strawberries, sugar, lemon juice, and time. The classical British jam, the way it sets without a sachet of pectin. About six jars from a kilo of fruit.
sourceNotes:
Drawn from three public-domain references and cross-checked against modern food-safety guidance:

• Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Chapter 26 on preserves and confectionery (Project Gutenberg #10136). Beeton's strawberry jam stays close to the open-pan method used here.
• Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), chapter on preserves (Project Gutenberg). Acton is sharper on the chemistry than Beeton, particularly on the role of acid in setting low-pectin fruit.
• USDA / National Center for Home Food Preservation, Complete Guide to Home Canning, Agriculture Information Bulletin 539. Public-domain US government guidance, used here to verify the safe acid ratio of strawberry plus lemon, and as the source for water-bath times in the cross-referenced canning tutorial.

The sugar ratio (800 g sugar to 1 kg fruit) is a touch lower than the standard 1:1 Beeton uses, in line with how British home cooks tend to make jam now. A firmer set comes at 1 kg to 1 kg; both are safe.

hero.alt: Three jars of finished ruby-red strawberry jam on a linen tea towel, a sprig of strawberry leaves to the side, a wooden spoon streaked with jam across one jar's rim, a halved lemon in the foreground.
hero.attribution: Illustration generated for Homemade using Flux 1.1 Pro Ultra, locked Modern Botanical style.

glossaryTerms.definition: A natural gum in fruit (highest in apple, citrus pith, redcurrant) that sets jam when boiled with sugar and acid. Strawberries are low in pectin, which is why this recipe leans on lemon juice.
The temperature and consistency at which a hot jam will set as it cools. Typically 104°C, confirmed with the wrinkle test on a frozen saucer.
To heat a clean glass jar to a temperature that kills the microbes left after washing. A low oven for 20 minutes is the home-kitchen standard.
The foam of denatured fruit proteins and air that rises during boiling. Skim it for a clearer jam. A small knob of butter at the start reduces how much forms.
To soak fruit in sugar (or salt, or acid) so that juice draws out by osmosis before any heat is applied.

content.text: This is a full strawberry-jam tutorial from market punnet to sealed jar. You will end up with about six 340 g jars from a kilo of fruit, ready in an afternoon.
Strawberry jam has a reputation for being temperamental, and the reason is in the fruit. Strawberries are low in
pectin

, the natural gum that sets jam, and they're high in water. Modern shortcuts add commercial pectin in a sachet; the open-pan method earns its set from the lemon and the boil instead. The flavour repays the patience: strawberries cooked fast in their own juice keep more of the fruit they started as.

You will need a wide pan, a sugar thermometer (useful, not essential), and a saucer in the freezer. The whole pan from cold-pour to sealed jar takes about an hour and a half, including the maceration.

attrs.title: Why this jam needs no sachet
attrs.body: Setting needs three things: pectin, sugar, and acid. Strawberries supply the sugar and most of the volume but very little pectin, and their acidity is moderate. The juice of one lemon brings the acidity up to where the pectin can knit, and the open-pan boil concentrates the fruit until the ratio is right. A 1:1 sugar-to-fruit ratio gives a firm set; the 800:1000 used here gives a softer set with more of the strawberry forward.

content.text: What you need

items.name: Strawberries, ripe
items.substitutions: frozen strawberries are fine, defrost first

items.name: Granulated sugar
items.substitutions: preserving sugar for a firmer set; not jam sugar with added pectin

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.qty: a teaspoon, optional, helps with scum

items.name: Heavy preserving pan
items.substitutions: any wide heavy-based pan, the wider the better

items.name: Jam funnel
items.substitutions: a wide ladle and a steady hand

items.name: Tea towel or tray
items.qty: 1, a stable surface to fill jars on

content.text: A word on safety

attrs.title: Hot sugar burns more deeply than boiling water
attrs.body: A pan of jam at setting point sits at 104°C and above, and the sugar holds heat in a way that water doesn't. A splash on bare skin will keep cooking after you brush it off. Long sleeves, the right pan, oven gloves at every transfer. If jam touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes. Take care, and get medical help if you need it.

content.text: Method
Step 1. Prepare the fruit

Hull the strawberries with the tip of a small knife. Halve any larger than a small plum; leave smaller ones whole. Weigh the hulled fruit on the scales, because punnets vary, and adjust the sugar to 80% of the hulled weight.
Step 2. Macerate

Layer fruit and sugar into a wide non-reactive bowl: a layer of fruit, a scatter of sugar, repeating until both are in. Cover with a clean cloth and leave for four hours on the counter, or overnight in the fridge. By the end the strawberries will sit in their own pink syrup. This step is the
maceration

attrs.title: Why macerate first
attrs.body: The sugar draws juice from the fruit before any heat arrives, by osmosis. The strawberries hold their shape better and the colour stays brighter once the pan goes on the heat. Skip the maceration and the jam still works, but you'll get a paler, mushier set.

content.text: Step 3. Sterilise the jars
Wash the jars and lids in hot soapy water and rinse well. Then
sterilise
the jars in the oven and the lids in a small saucepan:
Jars in a low oven (140°C / gas mark 1) for 20 minutes.
Lids in a small saucepan of just-boiled water for 10 minutes.
Leave both warm until you are ready to fill. Hot jam into cold jars cracks the glass; cold jam into hot jars wastes the sterilisation.
Step 4. Boil

Tip the macerated fruit and all of its syrup into the preserving pan. Add the lemon juice and the teaspoon of butter if using. Bring the pan to a simmer over a low heat, stirring with the wooden spoon, until every grain of sugar has dissolved. Run the spoon along the bottom and feel for grit: if you can still feel sugar, keep stirring.
Once the sugar has dissolved, turn the heat up and bring the pan to a hard rolling boil. The whole surface will heave and foam. Set a timer for 8 minutes.
Step 5. Test for setting point
When the timer goes, pull the pan off the heat and test for
setting point
. The frozen-saucer wrinkle test is the source of truth here; the thermometer is back-up.
The wrinkle test.

Drop a teaspoon of jam onto a saucer that has been in the freezer. Wait one minute. Push the blob with a fingertip. If the surface wrinkles like skin and the trail your finger leaves doesn't fill back in, the jam is set. If the blob slides smoothly and refills, it needs more boiling.
The flake test.
Lift a wooden spoon out of the pan and watch the drips. Jam at setting point falls as flat sheets that snap off cleanly. Jam not yet set falls as fast individual drops.
The thermometer.
104°C (220°F). Reliable for most jams, less reliable for strawberry, where the wrinkle test still wins. Use the thermometer as a sign to start testing, not as a verdict.

attrs.title: Take the pan off the heat each time you test
attrs.body: Strawberry jam can shoot past setting point in less than a minute at a hard boil. Every time you test on the saucer, lift the pan off the burner so it isn't overshooting while you wait. If the jam isn't set, put the pan back on and reboil for another two minutes before the next test.

content.text: Step 6. Skim and rest
Off the heat, skim the
scum
from the surface with the slotted spoon. A pale foam will have lifted to the top; lift it off and put it on a saucer. Some cooks eat it on toast. It is the cleanest spoonful in the kitchen.

Let the jam stand off the heat for five minutes. The strawberries redistribute through the jam during this rest. Skip it and the fruit floats to the tops of the jars; honour it and each jar sets with fruit suspended evenly.
Step 7. Jar and seal

Take the jars out of the oven onto a clean tea towel or board. Set the funnel in the first jar and ladle the jam in, leaving a 5 mm headspace at the top. Wipe each rim clean with a cloth dipped in just-boiled water; even a fingerprint of jam will spoil the seal.

Lift the hot lids from the saucepan with a teaspoon, fit each to a jar, and screw down firmly. As the jars cool you will hear a series of small clicks: the safety buttons on the lids pulling down as the air inside contracts. That click is the seal.

attrs.title: Sealing versus canning
attrs.body: A clean fill into a hot jar with a hot lid gives a vacuum seal good for about three months unopened. A water-bath process step extends the shelf life to twelve months sealed by killing any spores that the boil missed. For strawberry jam at this acid level, sealed-only is widely used in British home kitchens and supported by USDA guidance; if you are gifting, travelling, or storing for a year, water-bath process the jars.

content.text: Storage
Cool, dark cupboard, three months unopened.
Once opened, the fridge, four weeks.
Water-bath canned, sealed, twelve months in a cool dark cupboard.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: When the jar isn't right
attrs.intro: Most jam problems are recoverable in the pan. Mould in a sealed jar is the exception.

items.cause: Either it didn't reach setting point, or the fruit was lower in pectin than usual.
items.fix: Tip every jar back into the pan with the juice of another lemon, bring to a rolling boil, retest at 8 minutes. Re-sterilise the jars while the second boil runs. Some batches of strawberries are simply lower-pectin than others, and a second go fixes most of them.

items.cause: You overshot setting point. Strawberry jam overshoots fast at a rolling boil.
items.fix: Warm the contents of the jars in a pan with a splash of water; whisk smooth; re-jar while hot. Re-sterilise the jars first.

items.symptom: The strawberries floated to the top
items.cause: The jam went straight from pan to jar without the five-minute rest.
items.fix: Drink the visible-strawberry-layer top half on toast and use the looser jam underneath in cakes. Next batch, build five minutes' rest off the heat into the timing.

items.cause: Foam left on the jam at jarring time.
items.fix: Skim while the jam is still hot, before you fill the jars. A teaspoon of butter stirred in at the start of cooking dramatically reduces how much scum forms.

items.symptom: Sugar crystals on the surface or in the jar
items.cause: The sugar didn't fully dissolve before the rolling boil began.
items.fix: Salvage by warming the jar with a splash of water until the crystals dissolve; re-jar. Next time, hold the pan on a low heat until you cannot feel a single grain on the back of the spoon, then turn up.

items.symptom: Mould appeared on top of a sealed jar
items.cause: The seal failed. Either the jar wasn't sterile, the rim had jam on it, the lid had been used before, or the jar cooled in a draught.
items.fix: Bin the entire jar. Mould on jam isn't a 'cut around it' situation, because the visible bloom is one part of a mycelium that reaches through the soft fruit underneath.

content.text: Variations

attrs.label: the same method, four other fruits
attrs.heading: What else this technique covers
attrs.intro: The open-pan method works for most soft fruit. Adjust the sugar to 80% of the hulled weight, add the same lemon juice for low-pectin fruits, and use the same wrinkle test.

items.name: Raspberry
items.description: Same ratios. Raspberries break down further during the boil, so the finished jam is looser. A few of the pips inevitably end up on the spoon; sieve half the cooked fruit if you want a clearer set.

items.name: Gooseberry
items.description: Skip the lemon. Gooseberries bring their own acid and plenty of pectin. Tip them in whole; they collapse during the boil. The finished jam sets firm and tart.

items.name: Plum
items.description: Halve and stone the plums; tie the stones in a square of muslin and add to the pan for extra pectin, fishing them out before jarring. Half a lemon's worth of juice is plenty.

items.name: Blackcurrant
items.description: The strongest pectin of the four. Set comes fast and firm. Use a touch more sugar (900 g to 1 kg fruit) to balance the tartness, no lemon, and watch the rolling boil closely.

content.text: Where this jam lives

Most of it ends up on toast. The rest fills the middle of a Victoria sandwich, sits at the bottom of a Bakewell tart under the frangipane, gets spooned over plain yoghurt with toasted flaked almonds, runs through buttercream for sponge fillings, and stays in the fridge through the autumn to remind you of June.

attrs.quote: Of all the preserves which the housewife is called upon to make in the course of the year, the strawberry jam is, perhaps, the most welcome to the family.
attrs.attribution: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management, 1861

attrs.label: kit recommendation
attrs.title: Sugar thermometer, stainless dial face
attrs.description: Clips to the pan rim, reads in a single sweep, washes off cleanly. A digital probe works too, but the cheap stainless dial style holds calibration for years and survives drops. About £12 from any cookware shop. Optional kit — the wrinkle test is the final word, but the thermometer tells you when to start testing.
